segments.s041-g1: This is a temporal subordinate: فرستاده سلم (Salm's messenger) is the subject; چون گشت باز is an adverbial clause 'when/after he turned back/returned'. چون here functions as a classical 'when/as' connector; گشت (past of گشتن) means 'turned/returned' and باز is the adverb 'again/back'. Word order is subject + temporal clause.
segments.s041-g2: شهنشاه بنشست is a main clause: شهنشاه (the sovereign) + بنشست (past 'sat down'). The conjunction و links the next verb phrase بگشاد راز, where بگشاد is the past tense 'opened/revealed' (poetic/older form of گشود) and راز is its object 'secret'.
segments.s041-g3: گرامی جهانجوی را پیش خواند is a clause with object marker را: گرامی جهانجوی = 'the honored/world-seeking man' (title), را marks him as definite object, پیش 'before/in the presence of' indicates location, and خواند here means 'summoned/called' (classical use). Word order is [object + را] + locative + verb.
segments.s041-g4: همه گفتها پیش او بازراند is a clause reporting what was done: همه گفتها 'all the sayings/messages' is the object; پیش او 'before him' repeats the locative; بازراند is a narrative verb meaning 'relayed/related' here (an older or specialized verb). The sequence is object → locative → verb, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s041-g5: ورا گفت کان دو پسر جنگجوی: ورا 'to him' (dative/indirect object). گفت here introduces reported speech (classical past 'said/told'); کان is a classical connective introducing the content 'that' (here rendered 'that'/'as follows'); دو پسر جنگجوی is the subject of the reported clause 'two warrior sons'.
segments.s041-g6: ز خاور سوی ما نهادند روی is the reported clause: ز خاور 'from the east' (ز = poetic 'from'), سوی ما 'toward us', and نهادند (past plural of نهادن) + روی 'face' forms the idiom 'نهادند روی' = 'they turned their faces' (i.e. they directed/turned toward). Verb is past plural indicating completed action.
segments.s041-g7: از اختر چنین استشان بهره خود is a nominal predicate about fate: از اختر 'from the star(s)' marks astrological source; چنین 'thus' qualifies the fate; استشان is composed of است + شان 'is to them' (i.e. 'their lot is'); بهره خود 'their own share/lot' is the head noun phrase. Word order follows classical Persian predicative pattern.
segments.s041-g8: که باشند شادان به کردار بد is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that/which'. باشند is present-subjunctive/optative third-person plural here used in classical narration to state a characteristic ('who are' / 'that they be'); شادان 'joyful' is predicative; به کردار بد 'in/by bad conduct' is an adverbial phrase describing the manner. The clause explains the content of their allotted fate.
segments.s041-g9: دگر آنکه (literally “another that/which”) introduces an additional point: دگر = “another” and آنکه is a relative/introducing particle (‘that/which’). کشور is a noun “realm/country” with the 3rd‑person plural sense implied by the following predicate, and آبشخورست is a nominal predicate (آبشخور + است fused as آبشخورست) meaning “is a watering‑place/watering‑hole.” Word order is topic → predicate (poetic), so: “Another (thing) is that two realms are watering‑places.”
segments.s041-g10: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding statement (“which/that”). آن بومها را = “those homelands” with را as the definite/object/topic marker attached to بومها. درشتی is a quality (“harshness/roughness”) and برست is a past verbal form meaning here “came upon / made (become)” or broadly “caused to be” (poetic past). Together the clause says that this condition caused those lands to become harsh.
segments.s041-g11: برادرت is the noun برادر “brother” with the 2nd‑person possessive suffix -ت (“your brother”). چندان modifies برادر بود: چنان/چندان = “so/such (to that degree)” and بود is the past copula “was.” The phrase means “your brother was such (a) brother.”
segments.s041-g12: کجا here functions as a relative/temporal “where/when/that” tying this clause to the previous one. مر is the first‑person object/dative “to me,” ترا = “you” (object form, with -ت), را is the object/topic marker, and بر سر افسر بود is a locative expression “was on (the) head as (a) helmet” (بر = on/upon, سر = head, افسر = helmet). In literal order: “where to me you on the head a helmet were,” i.e. “where he put you as a helmet on my head.”
segments.s041-g13: چو = “when.” پژمرده شد is a compound past: پژمرده (withered/paled) + شد “became,” so “became withered/pale.” روی رنگین تو = “your rosy/colored face” with possessor تو after the noun (classical order). The whole clause: “When your rosy face becomes withered…”
segments.s041-g14: نگردد is the negative subjunctive/optative/poetic future of گشتن (ن‑ prefix + گردد) meaning “may not/will not turn/come.” دگر = “another/any other.” گرد here has the sense of one who comes about or visits (from the root meaning “turn/about”), بالین = “bedside/pillow (by)” and تو = “your.” Together: “no other will come about your bedside.” Note the negative prefix ن‑ attached to the verbal stem.
segments.s041-g15: تو‌گر (تو + گر) is a classical conditional particle meaning “if you …”. پیش شمشیر = “in the presence of / before the sword” (پیش = before/in front of, شمشیر = sword). مهرآوری is a verbal phrase (مهر + آوری) meaning “you bring/show affection/mercy” — here it is the verb of the protasis: “if you show mercy before the sword.”
segments.s041-g16: سرت = “your head” (سر + 2nd‑person enclitic). گردد is the present/subjunctive/future of گشتن used as “become” (گردد = will/become). آشفته = predicate adjective “disturbed/chaotic.” از داوری = a causal/ablative phrase “because of/by judgment” (از = from/because of, داوری = judgment). Together: “your head will become disturbed because of judgment.”
segments.s041-g25: نگه کرد is a compound where نگه (a noun 'gaze') combines with کرد (past-tense 'did/made') to yield 'cast a glance/looked'. The verb comes before the subject (poetic inversion); پس is a temporal/discourse adverb 'then'. ایرج نامور is the subject with a following epithet (name + adjective), i.e. 'Irāj the renowned.'
segments.s041-g26: برآن is the prepositional phrase 'at/on that' (here marking the object of the look). A string of adjectives (مهربان پاک فرخ) precedes the noun پدر and collectively modifies it: 'that kind, pure, fortunate father.' This whole phrase links as the object of نگه کرد from the previous segment.
segments.s041-g27: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic line: چنین 'thus', داد (here past tense of دادن) 'he gave', پاسخ 'an answer' — together 'thus he answered'. The word که introduces the reported speech, and ای شهریار is a vocative address 'O king' that begins the quotation.
segments.s041-g28: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (root 'look/gaze') + کن (imperative of کردن) = 'look!' or 'behold!'. بدین is the older fused prepositional form (به + این) meaning 'at/with this' and introduces the phrase گردش روزگار 'the turn/cycle of fate/time' as the object: 'look at this turning of fate.' (Note: the segment splits ب + دین in the provided tokens; دین here functions historically as 'این', so the provided lemma senses do not match exactly.)
segments.s041-g29: که introduces the subordinate clause ('for / that'), and چون باد forms a simile 'like the wind'. بر ما is the prepositional object 'over us', همی is a classical habitual/continuous marker (often 'keeps' or 'still'), and بگذرد is the present-subjunctive/present-form 'passes' used here in a general/habitual sense — 'passes over us like the wind.'
segments.s041-g30: خردمند مردم is a noun phrase meaning 'a wise person of the people' (مردم following خردمند gives a genitive/relational sense). چرا is the interrogative 'why' placed before the predicate, and غم خورد is a light-verb construction where خورد (here in the sense 'befall/suffer') combines with غم 'sorrow' to mean 'to suffer sorrow' — so 'why should a wise person suffer sorrow?'
segments.s041-g31: همی again gives a continuous or habitual aspect ('keeps / continually'), and پژمراند (poetic form) is a verbal idea 'withers/fades' — here describing action on رخ ارغوان 'the crimson face/cheek'. The verb precedes its subject (poetic inversion), so: 'the crimson face keeps fading.'
segments.s041-g32: کند is the causative/lexical verb 'makes/does' and here means 'causes to be' or 'darkens'. It takes the following noun phrase تیره دیدار روشن‌روان as its object: تیره 'dark' modifies دیدار 'meeting', which itself is qualified by روشن‌روان ('bright‑souled/cheerful') — 'it makes the meeting of the bright‑souled dark.'
segments.s041-g73: This is a compact noun phrase headed by the causative/prepositional particle ز (classical form of از) introducing the cause 'درد دل' (heart‑ache). The temporal adverb اکنون 'now' appears before the numeral+noun 'یکی نامه' ('one/a single letter'), and the possessor is given as the independent pronoun من rather than the later enclitic (نامه‌ام). Word order is classical: preposition + noun phrase + time + numeral + noun + free possessive.
segments.s041-g74: Two first‑person present verbs follow the noun phrase: نویسم and فرستم ('I write' and 'I send'). In older/poetic Persian these present forms carry an optative/indicative sense and are simply juxtaposed; the direct object (the letter) was stated in the previous segment. بدان is the prepositional phrase 'to that' built from ب (reduced form of به) + an old/dialectal element realized here as دان; it attaches to انجمن ('assembly'), which is the destination governed by فرستم.
segments.s041-g75: مگر here introduces a wish or hopeful supposition (classical/poetic use), followed by the adverb باز 'again' and the first‑person present verb بینم 'may I see'. ترا is the object 'you', written as the free 2nd‑person element ت plus the object marker را. تن درست is a noun + adjective phrase (noun first, adjective after, as is idiomatic in older Persian) meaning 'body intact/sound'.
segments.s041-g76: که introduces the result/purpose clause ('so that'). روشن روانم is a possessive phrase: روشن 'bright/clear' modifies روان with the first‑person suffix م (my soul/spirit). به دیدار is a prepositional expression 'at/to the sight/meeting of', and تست is an archaic/poetic form functioning as the copular/predicate element referring to 'you' (literally related to 'تو هست' in older usage); because the provided lexicon entry for تست does not match this archaic function, it is noted as irregular here. The clause literally says 'that my bright soul (is) at the sight of you.'
segments.s041-g17: دو فرزند من = 'my two children/sons': noun (فرزند) followed by the possessor من. کز is the fused relative 'کز' (archaic/poetic form of که + ز) introducing a relative phrase meaning 'who/that from'. دو دوش جهان is a poetic expression 'the two shoulders of the world' = the two corners/regions of the world.
segments.s041-g18: برینسان گشادند بر من زبان: گشادند is a past/plural perfective 'they opened' (classical/past narrative). زبان بر من = 'their tongue against me' (زبان = speech/utterance). برینسان here is a poetic/archaic form meaning roughly 'toward/among men' (the printed lexical option 'prince' does not fit well, so interpret contextually as a people/people-facing phrase); the preposition بر marks direction/target.
segments.s041-g19: گرت = contraction of اگر + تو (poetic) 'if you'; سر بکارست is a predicative phrase 'your head is for work' i.e. 'you are fit/capable for work' (سر = 'head' used metaphorically). بپسیچ کار is an imperative/commandive sequence where بپسیچ (reflexive/causative/archaic verb) = 'gird/fasten/wrap yourself' with کار 'the task' as its object.
segments.s041-g20: در گنج بگشای = imperative 'open the treasure' (بگشای = open). و = 'and', بربند بار = 'bind/secure the load' (بربند here is an imperative meaning 'fasten/tie up' and بار = cargo/load). Word order: verb follows object in the imperative sequence, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s041-g21: تو‌گر = 'if you' (poetic conditional). چاشت را = 'the noon meal' with را marking it as a definite object. دست یازی is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to lay hands on / to seize' (دست + یازی), here followed by به جام 'to the cup' — i.e. 'seize the noonday cup' (take your share).
segments.s041-g22: و‌گر نه = 'and if not / otherwise'. خورند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive classical form 'they eat' / 'they will eat'. ای پسر = vocative 'O son'. بر تو شام = 'dinner upon you' meaning 'they will eat (supper) at your expense' (بر indicates the target/beneficiary of the action).
segments.s041-g23: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'one should not / it ought not to be'. ز گیتی = 'from the world' (poetic for 'in this world'). ترا is the 2nd-person object clitic ('for you' / 'you (to have)'), placed before the noun it governs; یار کس = 'any friend/person' (negated by نباید). The clause expresses prohibition/advice: you ought not to have any companion in the world.
segments.s041-g24: بی‌آزاری و راستی یار بس = a nominal phrase giving the desired qualities: بی‌آزاری 'harmless / causing no harm' and راستی 'truthfulness / uprightness' qualify یار 'friend/companion'; بس is a final particle 'enough' meaning that such a harmless, truthful friend suffices. Word order lists the qualities before the noun they qualify.
segments.s041-g49: به بیهوده is an adverbial phrase placed up front: به here marks manner ('in/for') and بیهوده is an adverb meaning 'in vain/needlessly'. از شهریار زمین is a prepositional phrase 'at/from the king of the land' with از marking direction/target and شهریار زمین a noun compound where شهریار 'king' is specified by زمین 'the earth/land'. Together this phrase serves as the adverbial object/target of the verb that follows.
segments.s041-g50: مدارید is a second-person plural hortative/impositive form used here as an admonition ('refrain/spare' or 'do not do'), with an emphatic repetition for the two items that follow. خشم and کین are the two coordinated nouns governed by this verb ('anger' and 'vengeance/hatred'); و simply links them. The repetition مدارید … مدارید is a poetic device to insist on restraining both feelings.
segments.s041-g51: به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the world' (به + گیتی). مدارید again functions as an imperative/hortative 'do not place / do not rely on' in the continuing admonitory sequence. چندین امید ('several hopes' / 'many a hope') is the direct object of مدارید — literally 'set many hopes on the world.'
segments.s041-g52: نگر is an imperative 'look!/consider!' introducing the following clause. تا here introduces a subordinate clause giving the content/result ('to see/so that you may see'), چه is an interrogative/exclamative 'how/what', بد is an adjective 'bad', and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did' (3rd singular). با جمشید ('with Jamshid') is the prepositional agent/affected party placed after the verb — together: 'see what bad it did to Jamshid.'
segments.s041-g53: به فرجام هم is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'at the end / finally, too' (به + فرجام, with هم = 'also'). شد is the past-resultive 'happened/occurred' here ('it came to pass'). ز گیتی is a poetic genitive/prepositional 'from the world', and بدر (literally 'full moon') is used metaphorically for brightness/glory (here 'his moonlike radiance').
segments.s041-g54: نماندش is a past negative compound 'did not remain to him' (نماند + ـش = 'for him'). همان 'that same' points back to the previously named regalia: تاج (crown), تخت (throne) and کمر (here 'girdle/belt' of office). The sequence lists the items that were lost — the verb negates their continuing possession.
segments.s041-g55: مرا (here shown split as م + را) is the object 'me' with را the definite/object marker; 'با شما هم' means 'with you also/together with you'. به فرجام کار is a prepositional phrase 'to the end/outcome of the affair' (به + فرجام + کار). The whole segment sets up the experiencer and the circumstance for the verb in the next segment: 'I, too, with you, regarding the outcome of the matter…'.
segments.s041-g56: بباید is a compound modal 'it is necessary / one must' (here governing the speaker). چشیدن is the verbal noun 'to taste/experience' used as the object of بباید. بد روزگار is a fixed phrase 'bad fortune / ill days' (بد qualifying روزگار). Together: 'one must experience ill fortune' — here applied to the speaker as part of the shared fate.
segments.s041-g65: ولیکن (but) is a discourse connective introducing contrast. چو ... شود forms a temporal subordinate clause 'when ... becomes' with چو (when) preceding the subject جانی (a living being, here indefinite) and شود (becomes) as the verbal predicate. بی‌بها is a predicative adjective placed after the verb (typical poetic order) meaning 'priceless/invaluable.'
segments.s041-g66: نهد is a simple present (or narrative) verb 'places/puts' used transitively. پر خرد is a genitive-like phrase 'feather of wisdom' (پر = feather, خرد = wisdom) where پر is the head noun and خرد qualifies it. در دم اژدها is a locative phrase 'in/into the dragon's breath' (در = in; دم = breath/mouth of exhalation; اژدها = dragon) giving the place/occasion of the placing.
segments.s041-g67: چه پیش آیدش is a rhetorical clause 'what can happen to him' where چه is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what', پیش آیدش (lit. 'comes before him') = 'befalls him'. جز introduces an exception 'except'. گزاینده is an agent noun here meaning 'the biter/stinger' (one who bites or stings), and زهر specifies it as venomous/poisonous ('venom').
segments.s041-g68: کش here is an agentive/formal element meaning 'killer' or 'one that kills' (attached to the idea of biting/poisoning). از آفرینش 'from/by creation' states origin or nature. چنین است بهر is a verbless/finite clause in which چنین است = 'is thus' and بهر = 'for' or 'by nature' (classical usage), so the whole line says the killer is such by creation.
segments.s041-g69: ترا‌ای is a vocative/address 'to you, O ...' (ترا used emphatically). پسر‌گر is a vocative epithet 'father/begetter of sons' (used here to address the addressee). چنین است رای is an inverted predicate 'such is the counsel' (رای = counsel/plan); inversion is common in poetic style.
segments.s041-g70: بیارای here functions as an imperative or jussive 'arrange/put in order' (poetic usage of آراستن/آراييدن), governing the direct object کار ('affairs, the matter'). و connects to the second imperative بپرداز 'attend to / carry out'; جای 'place' means 'your post/position' or 'take your place' (object of بپرداز). The line gives two coordinated commands.
segments.s041-g71: پرستنده چند از میان سپاه is an interrogative/indefinite phrase 'how many followers/devotees from among the army' where چند = 'how many' (or 'some' depending on context), از میان = 'from among', and سپاه = 'the army/host.'
segments.s041-g72: بفرمای is a narrative or polite reporting verb 'he said/commanded.' کایند is an archaic relative/verb-form meaning 'who are / who should be' (here introducing which of them are to accompany). با تو به راه is a prepositional phrase 'with you on the road' (با = with, تو = you, به راه = on the way).
segments.s041-g57: Word order: Topic/object 'دل' (heart) comes first, followed by the noun of feeling 'کینه' (hatred) and the directional clitic/compound 'ورشان' (toward them). 'بدین' is the fused form of به + این, here an adverbial 'thus / into this' modifying the verb. 'آورم' is first-person singular present/subjunctive of آوردن used here in a modal sense 'I bring / I should bring / I put'. Together: 'I put/bring hatred into my heart toward them.'
segments.s041-g58: 'سزاوارتر' is the comparative adjective 'more fitting/appropriate'. 'زانکه' introduces the subordinate clause and here functions in a comparative sense ('than that' / classical marker linking the comparison). 'کین' is the object 'vengeance' and 'آورم' repeats the first-person verb 'I bring/perform'. The clause literally reads 'more fitting than that I bring vengeance,' i.e. '...rather than exact vengeance.'
segments.s041-g59: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' with the indirect-object clitic/phrase 'بدو' (to him) placed before the verb, a common classical order. What follows is the vocative/address: 'شاه‌ای خردمند پور' — 'O king/royal one, O wise son' (an honorific appellation). 'پور' is an appositional element 'son/descendant' used in names and addresses.
segments.s041-g60: Vocative 'برادر' (brother) introduces the address. 'همی' is a particle indicating ongoing/regular action or emphasis (here 'indeed/continually'). 'رزم جوید' is a verb phrase 'seeks battle' (جویدن governs رزم as its object). 'تو' is the emphatic/explicit subject 'you'; 'سور' (feast) is coordinated as another object: 'you seek battle and (you seek) feasting.' Poetic word order allows the verb before or between its objects.
segments.s041-g61: The sequence 'مرا این سخن یاد باید گرفت' should be read as 'مرا' (me / for me) + 'این سخن' (this saying) + 'یاد باید گرفت' (must be learned/remembered). Classical Persian often places the object(s) before the modal 'باید' and the verbal form 'گرفت' (here infinitive/past form used in a periphrastic construction). The whole means 'I must remember/learn these words.'
segments.s041-g62: Poetic prepositional phrase: 'ز' = 'from/of', 'مه' = 'moon', 'روشنایی' = 'brightness/light'. 'نیاید' is the negative present 'does not come/appear'. 'شگفت' (literally 'wonder') is predicative; the clause 'ز مه روشنایی نیاید شگفت' reads idiomatically as 'it is no wonder that light does not come from the moon' (i.e. unsurprising that the moon gives no light here).
segments.s041-g63: 'ز تو' = 'from you'. 'پر خرد' is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' (پر = full (of) + خرد = wisdom) modifying 'پاسخ' (answer). 'ایدون' is an older/poetic present form 'came/comes' (cf. آید), and 'سزید' is a parenthetical/predicate meaning 'it was fitting/it merited / it was appropriate.' The clause reads 'From you came an answer full of wisdom; fittingly so.'
segments.s041-g64: 'دلت' is 'دل' + second-person suffix '-ت' = 'your heart'. 'مهر پیوند' is a compound: 'مهر' = love/affection and 'پیوند' = bond/union (i.e. love of alliance). 'ایشان' = 'them/they' (object of the bond), and 'گزید' is simple past 'chose'. Word order places the possessive 'دلت' first; the clause means 'Your heart chose (the) love of union with them.'
segments.s041-g33: به آغاز places the time/location 'at the beginning' (به + noun), followed by گنج است (copula است = 'there is treasure'). The second clause 'فرجام رنج' omits the copula (elliptical), so it reads 'and (the) end (is) suffering' — classical Persian commonly drops است in the second coordinate.
segments.s041-g34: پس از رنج is an adverbial phrase 'after suffering' (پس = 'after' here + از). رفتن is the infinitive/verb 'to go/leave' placed before its agent (poetic inversion); ز جای سپنچ 'from the place of the sponge' gives the origin/direction. Read together: 'after suffering, (it) goes away from the sponge's place' (سپنچ is the subject/thing that moves).
segments.s041-g35: چو introduces a conditional/temporal 'if/when'. بستر ز خاکست = 'a bed of ashes' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic diction). The parallel phrase بالین ز خشت = 'a pillow of brick' — two parallel nominal phrases linked by و. This is a typical conditional setup in classical Persian.
segments.s041-g36: درختی is the noun 'a tree'. چرا is the interrogative 'why'. باید is the modal 'must / ought', and امروز is the temporal 'today'. کشت here functions as the verbal infinitive/noun 'to plant / planting' (بعد از باید the bare stem expresses the action). Together: 'why ought one today to plant a tree?'
segments.s041-g37: که introduces an explanatory or relative clause ('for/that'). هر چند here means 'however often / however many times' (a fixed quantifier + چند). چرخ is used in the sense of fate/wheel of fortune, and از برش بگذرد = 'pass over/from his wound' (بگذرد is the present/subjunctive 'may pass' used for general or potential occurrence).
segments.s041-g38: تن + ش is 'his body' (تن = body, ش = his). خون خورد is a figurative collocation 'drank blood' (خورد used like 'to take/drink/receive'); بار کین آورد = 'brought/caused the burden of vengeance' (بار = burden/load, کین = vengeance, آورد = brought/caused). The clause states the bodily consequence and the coming of enmity.
segments.s041-g39: This is a nominal epithet: خداوند followed by three nouns in apposition (شمشیر و گاه و نگین) — 'lord/master of sword, of time/occasion, and of gem'. The sequence names attributes over which the lord is sovereign; no verb in this segment.
segments.s041-g40: چو = 'when/as'. ما دید is a simple past 'he saw us'. بسیار here modifies دید 'saw much/many things' (or 'saw greatly'), and و بیند زمین places a second vision verb (بیند 'beholds/sees') with زمین as its object — a poetic pairing of past and present/perfective verbs meaning he both looked upon us and laid eyes on the earth. Persian poetry often juxtaposes verbs this way for emphasis.
segments.s041-g41: از ... پیش here groups several elements: از ('of/among') introduces a subset ('among those'), آن 'that/those' points to the previously mentioned group, تاجور is a noun 'merchant', and نامداران is an epithet 'renowned/illustrious'. پیش placed at the end gives the temporal sense 'before/previously'. Together the phrase means 'previously among those famed merchants'.
segments.s041-g42: ندیدند is the past tense, third‑person plural 'they did not see / had not seen'. کین 'vengeance/hatred' is the object. اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within', آیین is 'custom/practice', and خوی + ش is the possessive 'their own' (خوی = habit/custom, ش = possessive pronoun). The clause means 'they had not before seen vengeance in their own practice.'
segments.s041-g43: چو is a conditional 'if/when'. دستور is 'an order/command'. باشد is the subjunctive/present form of 'to be' used here to mean 'there is / (if) there be'. م + را together function as the object marker 'me' (مرا), marking the recipient of the order, and شهریار is the agent 'the king'. Word order is inverted, so: 'if there is an order to me from the king' → 'if the king orders me'.
segments.s041-g44: به بد is an adverbial phrase 'into/unto ill / in an evil way'. نگذرانم is a first‑person singular negative present/subjunctive meaning 'I will not let (something) pass / I will not permit'. بد روزگار is the object phrase 'bad fortune / ill days'. Together: 'I will not allow ill fortune to come upon me.'
segments.s041-g45: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / it ought not' (expressing prohibition or duty). م + را again combine to form 'مرا' (the person affected: 'me' / 'as for me'). تاج و تخت و کلاه lists regalia 'crown and throne and cap' (three items in apposition). The whole segment expresses 'it is not right that I (while wearing) crown, throne, and cap...'.
segments.s041-g46: شوم is first‑person singular subjunctive 'I become / I be' used here to express the state 'if I become / if I am (so)'. پیش ایشان means 'before them / in their presence'. دوان is a participial/adverbial form 'running / rushing', and بی‌سپاه is 'without an army / unescorted'. Together: '…to run before them without troops.'
segments.s041-g47: بگویم is first‑person present/subjunctive used as an expression of speaking 'I say / shall I say'. که‌ای is the combination introducing direct address: که (that/which) with ای as the vocative particle, so here it introduces 'O ...'. نامداران is the vocative 'renowned/illustrious ones' and من after it functions as a possessive ('my' in classical word order). The phrase is a direct address: 'I say: O my illustrious ones…'.
segments.s041-g48: چنان چون is a correlative pair meaning 'such as / so that / thus as', introducing a comparison or degree. گرامی is an adjective 'dear/precious', and تن و جان من means 'my body and my soul/life' (note the possessive 'من' follows the noun as in classical Persian: 'my'). The clause states the speaker's valuation: 'my body and my life are so precious.'

sentences.s041-s1: When Salm's messenger returned, the king sat down and unveiled the secret.
sentences.s041-s2: He summoned the honored world-seeker before him and recited all the words to him.
sentences.s041-s3: He told him that two warrior sons had turned their faces from the east toward us.
sentences.s041-s4: From the stars their lot is thus: that they be joyful in bad conduct.
sentences.s041-s5: Another thing is that two realms are watering-places, which made those homelands grow harsh.
sentences.s041-s6: Your brother was such a brother that he placed you as a helmet upon my head.
sentences.s041-s7: When your fair face withers, no other will come about your bedside.
sentences.s041-s8: If you show mercy before the sword, your head will be thrown into disarray by judgment.
sentences.s041-s13: Then famed Irāj cast his gaze upon that kind, pure, fortunate father.
sentences.s041-s14: Thus he answered, 'O king, look at this turn of fate.'
sentences.s041-s15: For, like the wind, it passes over us — why should a wise person of the people suffer sorrow?
sentences.s041-s16: The crimson cheek keeps fading; it darkens the meeting of the bright‑souled.
sentences.s041-s37: From the pain of my heart I now write a single letter of mine and send it to that assembly.
sentences.s041-s38: May I see you again in sound body, so that my clear spirit may be brightened at your sight.
sentences.s041-s9: My two sons, who from the two shoulders (corners) of the world have opened their tongues against me.
sentences.s041-s10: If you have a head for work, gird yourself to the task: open the treasure and bind (fasten) the load.
sentences.s041-s11: If you can lay your hand on the noonday cup, do so; otherwise, O son, others will eat (dinner) at your expense this evening.
sentences.s041-s12: You should have no friend in the world except one who is harmless — an honest/upright friend is enough.
sentences.s041-s25: Do not, without cause, turn your anger and your hatred against the king of the land.
sentences.s041-s26: Do not place many hopes on the world; consider how ill it treated Jamshid.
sentences.s041-s27: At the end, even the moonlike glory departed from the world; that very crown, throne and girdle did not remain for him.
sentences.s041-s28: I too, along with you, must in the end of affairs taste ill fortune.
sentences.s041-s33: But when a living being becomes priceless, he sets a feather of wisdom into the dragon's breath.
sentences.s041-s34: What can happen to him except the venomous biter? Such is the killer by creation.
sentences.s041-s35: To you, O father of sons, such is the counsel: set your affairs in order and take your place.
sentences.s041-s36: How many devoted followers from among the army? He ordered which should go with you on the road.
sentences.s041-s29: It is more fitting that I put hatred into my heart toward them than that I take vengeance.
sentences.s041-s30: The king said to him, 'O wise son — brother, you indeed seek battle and feasting.'
sentences.s041-s31: I must learn/remember these words; it is no wonder that the moon gives no light.
sentences.s041-s32: From you came an answer full of wisdom — it was fitting; your heart chose love of union with them.
sentences.s041-s17: At the beginning there is treasure, and at the end there is suffering; after suffering, the sponge leaves its place.
sentences.s041-s18: If the bed is of ashes and the pillow of brick, why should one plant a tree today?
sentences.s041-s19: For though the wheel may pass over his wound many times, his body will drink blood and bring the burden of vengeance.
sentences.s041-s20: The lord of sword and time and gem — when he saw us, he saw much and beheld the earth.
sentences.s041-s21: Even those famed merchants had not before seen such hatred in their own customs.
sentences.s041-s22: If the king gives me an order, I will not let ill fortune befall me.
sentences.s041-s23: I ought not, wearing crown, throne, and cap, to run before them unescorted.
sentences.s041-s24: Shall I say, 'O my renowned ones, I value my body and my life thus'?
